skill.name: architecture-decision-records
skill.description: Write and maintain Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) following best practices for technical decision documentation. Use when documenting significant technical decisions, reviewing past architect...
skill.summary: Write and maintain Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) following best practices for technical decis...
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Security analysis confirms this is a safe documentation skill. Static findings for external_commands, network, and weak_crypto are false positives. The skill contains markdown templates, example CLI commands (shown as documentation), and reference URLs to external documentation. No executable code, no data exfiltration, no cryptographic operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: External Commands Detection
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' but this is a documentation skill. Lines 55-59 show ASCII art arrows in ADR lifecycle diagram. Lines 374-394 show example adr-tools CLI commands as documentation, not actual execution. These are safe documentation patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: ASCII arrows in markdown lifecycle diagram and CLI command examples in documentation are not actual code execution

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Network Detection
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 'Hardcoded URLs' but these are legitimate documentation reference links to PostgreSQL docs, Martin Fowler articles, and ADR project resources. No network requests are made by this skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Reference URLs in markdown are static documentation links, not network threat vectors

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' 11 times but this is a pure documentation skill. No cryptographic operations exist. Detection likely triggered by 'MADR' (Markdown ADR) format or similar patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a documentation skill with no cryptographic code or operations whatsoever

content.value_statement: This skill helps teams document significant technical decisions with structured templates. It provides comprehensive ADR formats, lifecycle management guidance, and best practices for capturing context, trade-offs, and rationale.
actual_capabilities: Generate Standard ADR documents using MADR format with context, decision drivers, considered options, and consequences
Create lightweight ADRs for quick decisions with status, date, and concise format
Produce Y-Statement format ADRs for brief architectural choices
Document ADR deprecation and supersession with migration plans and lessons learned
Provide RFC-style ADRs for complex technical proposals with detailed design sections
limitations: Does not execute or validate technical decisions - only provides documentation templates
Does not integrate with project management tools or version control systems
Does not generate code or implement architectural patterns
Does not provide decision analysis or recommendations

use_cases.title: Documenting Database Technology Selection
use_cases.description: When choosing between PostgreSQL, MySQL, or MongoDB for a new project, use the Standard ADR template to capture requirements, compare options, and record the decision with full rationale.
use_cases.target_user: Software architects and technical leads making technology stack decisions

use_cases.title: Recording Framework Migration Decisions
use_cases.description: When planning to migrate from Angular to React, create a deprecation ADR to document the reasons for change, migration phases, and lessons learned from the previous decision.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering managers and team leads overseeing technology transitions

use_cases.title: Establishing Team Decision Processes
use_cases.description: When onboarding new team members, use the ADR templates to help them understand past architectural choices and the reasoning behind current architecture.
use_cases.target_user: Senior developers and architects responsible for team knowledge transfer

prompt_templates.title: Basic ADR Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Architecture Decision Record for [DECISION_TITLE]. Use the Standard MADR format. Include context about why we need to make this decision, the options we considered, our final decision, and the consequences.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick request for documenting a straightforward technical decision

prompt_templates.title: Technology Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an ADR comparing [TECHNOLOGY_A], [TECHNOLOGY_B], and [TECHNOLOGY_C] for our [USE_CASE]. Include detailed pros and cons for each option, our requirements, and recommend the best choice with rationale.
prompt_templates.scenario: When evaluating multiple technology options for a specific use case

prompt_templates.title: Deprecation Record
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a deprecation ADR for [OLD_TECHNOLOGY] which is being replaced by [NEW_TECHNOLOGY]. Include the context of why the original decision was made, what changed, the migration plan, and lessons learned.
prompt_templates.scenario: Documenting the deprecation of an existing technology or approach

prompt_templates.title: RFC-Style Proposal
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a detailed RFC-style ADR for [PROPOSAL]. Include motivation, detailed design, drawbacks, alternatives considered, unresolved questions, and implementation plan. Make it suitable for team review and discussion.
prompt_templates.scenario: Complex technical proposal requiring thorough analysis and team review

output_examples.input: Create an ADR for adopting TypeScript for our frontend project
output_examples.output: A complete ADR document with Status: Accepted, Context about current React codebase challenges, Decision to adopt TypeScript, Consequences covering good (type safety, IDE support) and bad (learning curve, build complexity), and Mitigations including training sessions.

output_examples.input: Document the deprecation of our legacy authentication service
output_examples.output: A deprecation ADR with status showing it supersedes ADR-0015, Context listing issues with the current service, Migration Plan with phased approach, and Lessons Learned about what went wrong with the original decision.

best_practices: Write ADRs before implementation begins to capture the decision context accurately
Include honest trade-offs and potential downsides in the consequences section
Link related ADRs to build a decision dependency graph for the project
Update ADR status when decisions are deprecated or superseded
anti_patterns: Writing vague decisions without specific context or rationale
Skipping the alternatives section to avoid showing other options were considered
Changing accepted ADRs instead of creating new ones to supersede
Creating ADRs for minor decisions that do not warrant architectural documentation

faq.question: What is an Architecture Decision Record?
faq.answer: An Architecture Decision Record (ADR) is a document that captures important architectural decisions made during a project. It typically includes the context, the decision itself, and the consequences of that decision.

faq.question: When should I create an ADR?
faq.answer: Create an ADR when making significant architectural decisions such as framework adoption, database technology choice, API design patterns, security architecture, or integration patterns. Skip ADRs for minor patches or routine maintenance.

faq.question: What ADR format should I use?
faq.answer: Use the Standard MADR format for comprehensive decisions, Lightweight format for quick decisions, Y-Statement for brief choices, and RFC-style for complex proposals requiring team review.

faq.question: How do I link related ADRs?
faq.answer: Include a Related Decisions section in each ADR that references other ADRs by number. Use status indicators like 'Supersedes ADR-0003' or 'Complements ADR-0002' to show relationships.

faq.question: Can I modify an accepted ADR?
faq.answer: No. Accepted ADRs should never be modified. Instead, create a new ADR that supersedes the old one and document what changed and why.

faq.question: What tools can help manage ADRs?
faq.answer: adr-tools is a popular CLI tool for managing ADRs. It can initialize directories, create new ADRs, generate table of contents, and link related decisions.
